slides.title: One shared slide body
slides.emphasis: Single source of truth for what the room sees.
slides.trainerCadence:
Welcome—let's take a minute to settle. I'm going to name three modes we use in Launch—presentation, trainer, and participant—and where each one usually lives: the projector screen everyone sees, your private trainer view, and each person's own device.

You don't need to decide your whole workflow right now; I just want the map in your head.
slides.trainerTransition: When that feels clear enough, go ahead and advance—we'll keep this as setup, not a long debate.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Calm, practical tone; reduce tech anxiety.
- Name projector vs laptop vs phone explicitly.
- Watch for people who feel behind—reassure them.
promptQuestions: Where do you expect to use each mode this week?
What would distract you on the projector screen?
slides.transitionCue: When heads nod, advance—this is setup, not debate.
slides.interaction: With a partner: which mode will you use first when you rehearse this at home?

slides.title: Participant mode: reflection
slides.trainerCadence:
I'm going to give you a full minute of quiet—maybe ninety seconds—just to write what you're actually thinking. You don't owe anyone a polished sentence yet.

If your mind goes blank, start with this: "What I'm feeling about partnership language is…"
slides.trainerTransition: When time's up, I'll invite one or two brief shares if the room has energy for it—then we'll move on.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Protect silence; resist filling it.
- Offer one starter phrase only if several people look frozen.
- Keep shares short if you open the mic.
promptQuestions: Who heard something worth stealing from a neighbor?
slides.transitionCue: Invite one or two brief shares, then move on.
slides.interaction: In one or two sentences: what tension do you feel between partnership and “fundraising” language?

slides.title: Participant mode: fill-in
slides.trainerCadence:
You'll see blanks on your screen—I'm going to read the instructions once, slowly, and then I want you to fill them in with your own words. There isn't a trick answer here; I'm after honest wording.

Give yourself permission to be plain. Simple language usually means you actually believe it.
slides.trainerTransition: If you have extra time, you can pair up and read your lines to each other—but we can also move on cleanly.
slides.trainerScriptNotes:
- Read prompt once; avoid commentary while they write.
- Normalize messy drafts.
- Optional pair share only if time allows.
slides.transitionCue: Optional: pair share using their completed sentence.
slides.interaction: Complete the sentence in your own words.

interactionData.prompt: Partnership Development is about raising ________, not merely raising ________.
interactionData.helperText: Plain language is fine—use words that feel true to you.
